coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots incorporated UiO-66-NH2 as a promising photocatalyst for degradation of long-chain oleic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Safa, Saeed
Khajeh, Mostafa
Reza Oveisi, Ali
Azimirad, Rouhollal
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) incorporated UiO-66-NH2 MOF was deposited on a steel foam for photocatalytic degradation of a long-chain oleic-acid. To have a better adhesion and homogenous distribution of MOF particles, the surface of steel foam was precoated by a thin film of ZnO. The formation of heterojunctions, crystallinity, morphology, particle size, proper separation of excitons, and elemental contents were examined by FTIR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, BET, PL and contact angle tests. Interestingly, the photoluminescence study revealed that the heterojunctions completely extinct the UiO-66-NH2 characteristic peaks at 450 and 670 nm via the electron/hole recombination inhibition. The experiments showed that the increased density of photogenerated electron/hole pairs resulted in a very fast degradation of organic structure of the oleic acid to small chain and less-hydrophobic species. In addition, the AISI steel/ZnO/UiO-66-NH2/GQDs showed completely hydrophilic characteristic at ~ 3 h irradiation, while the intrinsic AISI steel/ZnO sample was needed to a continuous irradiation for 7 h to decrease the water contact angle from ~ 76° to ~ 45°. The results were repeated for three times and showed only 10% decrease in the photocatalytic performance. The improved photocatalytic performance was mainly assigned to the charge transfer between UiO-66-NH2 and the GQD which could inhibit the photogenerated electron/hole recombination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UiO-66-NH2
Graphene quantum dot
Photocatalytic properties
Degradation
Contact angle

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The four various studied samples with respect to fabrication arrangement. The schematic of fabrication is associated with each one.

(a) SEM image and (b) AFM micrograph of the as-synthesized GQDs dispersed on a piece of mica wafer. The inset shows height profilomtery of the dashed white line. (c) TEM image and the corresponding size-distribution of the GQDs. The inset shows the high-resolution TEM image containing a typical inter-layer distance. (d) Optical transmittance and (e) FTIR and (f) XRD spectra of the as-prepared GQDs.
(a) SEM image and (b) FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized UiO-66-NH2 powder.

SEM images of (a) ZnO thin film/AISI steel mesh and (b) UiO-66-NH2/ ZnO thin film/AISI steel mesh. The insets show higher magnified images. (c) The schematic layout of the proposing mechanism of bonding the layers. (d) The elemental map of majors (Zr, O, Zn and Fe) corresponding the UiO-66-NH2/ZnO thin film/AISI steel mesh sample. (e) XRD patterns of the composite sample. The inset attributed to the PXRD of the as-prepared UiO-66-NH2 (a is related to the real PXRD and b is related to the simulated one).
Water contact angle of (a) bare AISI 304 steel mesh, (b) ZnO thin film/AISI steel mesh and (c) UiO-66-NH2/ZnO thin film/AISI steel mesh.

(a) Raman spectrum of the GQDs/UiO-66-NH2/ZnO/AISI 304 steel and (b) photoluminscence spectra of the samples. The inset shows the PL spectrum of as-prepared GQDs suspension at various excitation wavelengths.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism of the UiO-66-NH2/GQDs.
Time evolution of water contact angle of oleic acid deposited samples. The optical pictures of water droplets are shown in some stages.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots incorporated UiO-66-NH2 as a promising photocatalyst for degradation of long-chain oleic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Mostafa Khajeh, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, P. O. Box: 98615-538, Zabol, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) incorporated UiO-66-NH2 MOF was deposited on a steel foam for photocatalytic degradation of a long-chain oleic-acid. To have a better adhesion and homogenous distribution of MOF particles, the surface of steel foam was precoated by a thin film of ZnO. The formation of heterojunctions, crystallinity, morphology, particle size, proper separation of excitons, and elemental contents were examined by FTIR, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, BET, PL and contact angle tests. Interestingly, the photoluminescence study revealed that the heterojunctions completely extinct the UiO-66-NH2 characteristic peaks at 450 and 670 nm via the electron/hole recombination inhibition. The experiments showed that the increased density of photogenerated electron/hole pairs resulted in a very fast degradation of organic structure of the oleic acid to small chain and less-hydrophobic species. In addition, the AISI steel/ZnO/UiO-66-NH2/GQDs showed completely hydrophilic characteristic at ~ 3 h irradiation, while the intrinsic AISI steel/ZnO sample was needed to a continuous irradiation for 7 h to decrease the water contact angle from ~ 76° to ~ 45°. The results were repeated for three times and showed only 10% decrease in the photocatalytic performance. The improved photocatalytic performance was mainly assigned to the charge transfer between UiO-66-NH2 and the GQD which could inhibit the photogenerated electron/hole recombination.

ce:section.ce:para: Pointing to the recent environmental problems of organic pollutants, photocatalysts have been considered as one of the promising candidates to degrade the undesired compound under light source and to overcome such problems. Up to now, semiconductor-based photocatalysts have been emerged as the most practical, reliable and efficient materials [1] for photocatalytic degradation of various organic pollutants [1–4]. The research has shown that the self-cleaning behavior of a photocatalyst is an important property for the enhanced performance. Since, the ultraviolet portion of sunlight is only ~ 5% of solar irradiation [5,6], however, researchers have been focused on visible-light assisted photocatalysis for better utilization of solar energy [7,8]. Among the various types of materials, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), as the most advanced class of porous 3D materials, have been used as the best nominee for catalytic and photocatalytic applications [9–11], adsorption and removal of heavy metals and toxic chemicals [12–15]. This is mainly, due to: a) having an optical band-gap related to the charge transfer between ligands and metal-oxo clusters, b) having amazing high surface area, and c) possibility of their functionality and their activity modulation. For example, photocatalytic behaviors of MOFs have been studied in photodegradation of methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), norfloxacion, and hydrogen production [16,17].

Besides, carbonaceous nano-structures have been recognized as very prominent additive to the bare photocatalysts to harvest visible light and to inhibit photogenerated electron/hole recombination [3,18]. Hence, a considerable number of publications have been conducted on graphene-based composites [19,20]. For instance, these materials have been applied for hydrogen production [17] and degradation of organic dyes [21]. Along with this, graphene in the form of quantum dot [22] has been attained plenty of attention due to the high surface area [23], the high absorption capacity in the visible region [24] and the inhibition of photocatalytic corrosion [23].

Oleic acid is a liquid compound with low volatility and high stability which can be used to simulate the grease stains on various types of surfaces. It is used for testing the self-cleaning capability of a surface based-on the draft international standards [25]. The best result of Rathusky et al [25] against oleic acid was a water contact angle of about 7° after 30 h of irradiation (2000 uW/cm2 of UV-A) on TiO2 thin film. In another work, Fateh et al. [26], found that the better photocatalytic performance against oleic acid was a mixed layer of ZnO/TiO2 in which the water contact angle was reduced from 85° to less than 5° during 18 h of 365 nm irradiation (intensity of 1000 uW/cm2).

Hence, in this survey, a solar-light assisted photodegradation of a long-chain molecules of oleic acid on the permeable structure of steel mesh/ZnO/UiO-66/GQDs was designed and studied. The crystalline structure and optical properties of the material were evaluated and the effect of GQDs on the photocatalytic performance of the samples was assessed and discussed. Based on our knowledge, this is first study on the photocatalytic properties of UiO-66-NH2 MOF by addition of graphene quantum dots and the synthesis of such composite which is the main objective of our work.

All of the precursors were in analytical grade and used as purchased without further purification. The materials used in this experiments were NaOH (Merck 106498), ethanol (Merck 100971–2500), ZrCl4 (Merck 10026–11-6), N,Nʹ-dimethylformamide (DMF, Merck 68–12-2), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC 1
1 1,4-Benzenedicarboxylic acid (Terephthalic acid). -NH2, Sigma-Aldrich 10312–55-7), zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO2)•2H2O, Merck 108802) and diethanolamine (DEA, Merck 803116). The graphite electrodes were selected and cut from high pure graphite plates widely using as the heating element.

The graphene quantum dot suspension was synthesized according to the method developed by Li et al. [27]. In summary, two graphitic plate electrodes (length of 20 mm and width of 10 mm) were thoroughly washed with acetone and DI water and placed within a 50 mL beaker at a constant distance of 20 mm. In another beaker, a composition of 380 mg of NaOH, 1 mL DI water and 20 mL of ethanol were mixed and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Afterward, the clear solution was added to the former beaker containing graphite electrodes while the solution covers 10 mm of the electrodes. A direct current potential of 25 V was applied to the electrodes for 40 min. Afterward, the darkened solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 4 h to separate the thinned quantum dots of graphene sheets from thicker ones.

For the synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 samples, the protocol reported by Katz et al. [28] was used. At first, 0.54 mmol of ZrCl4 was added to 1 mL of HCl and ultrasonicated up to complete dissolution. Afterward, 5 mL of N,Nʹ-dimethylformamide (DMF) was added to the glass vial and ultrasonicated for 15 min. Moreover, 0.75 mmol of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2) was added to the prepared solution and once again, 10 mL of DMF was dripped to the solution, then capped and sonicated for 10 min. In order to crystalize the Zr6O4(OH)4(BDC-NH2)6 nanostructure, the supersaturated solution was aged at 80 °C for 12 h. The yellowish white suspension was filtered and subsequently washed several times with DMF and ethanol, respectively. After that, the trapped solvents were entirely vaporized by keeping the powder at 120 °C.

The last stage of this research stands on how to apply UiO-66-NH2 samples as photocatalyst against oleic acid. As we focused to fabricate a practical photocatalyst filter that not only traps the impurities inside the MOF’s porosities but also degrades them to small molecules under visible-light irradiation. So, the 0.8 mm thick AISI 304 stainless steel mesh plate (mesh number of 2400) as the substrates were cut into to square pieces of 20 mm pieces and washed thoroughly by ethanol, acetone and DMF, respectively. In this investigation, four types of samples were prepared as schematically shown in Fig. 1 . For the deposition of ZnO thin film, ZnO sol was prepared by dissolving zinc acetate in ethanol (0.5 M) and subsequently adding diethanolamine (DEA) to the solution with molar ratio of DEA/ZnAc = 6/5. Then, the resultant solution was stirred at 333 K for 30 min to yield a transparent and homogeneous sol which was considered for the dip-coating process. The pre-washed AISI 304 steel meshes were coated by dip-coating procedure. Afterward, the samples were calcinated at 350 °C for 60 min under 250 sccm of nitrogen to overcome any unwanted oxidation. Subsequently, the samples were withdrawn in a 0.15 g/L of UiO-66-NH2/DMF suspension and stirred slowly for 24 h. The prepared samples were then dried at 50 °C. Finally, the samples were washed with DI water slowly to remove the free and weak-bonded particles. Finally, a 5 mL of the 0.1 g/cc of GQD suspension was sprayed on each sample at a fixed distance of 10 cm and samples dried at 100 °C for 24 h. To have a better connection for charge transfer, the samples were withdrawn in ethanol and irradiated continuously with a 350 mW/cm2 UV-A [29].

The crystalline structure of the as-prepared ZnO nano-materials was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique using an Inel. EQUNOX 3000 with Cu-kα radiation source at λ = 1.54056 Å. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA TESCAN II) was employed to study the size and morphology of the prepared samples. TEM images were obtained by a Philips CM30 transmission electron microscope. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of samples were obtained using a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr pellets. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were taken with a Micromeritics TriStar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer at liquid nitrogen. The Raman spectroscopy is another useful tool to confirm the co-existence of graphene quantum dot and UiO-66 powder in the samples. To avoid fluorescence of the samples [30], the samples excited at 785 nm by using a SENTERRA (Bruker) apparatus was applied. Aiming to the importance of the evaluation of the electronic interactions between UiO-66-NH2 and GQDs, a photoluminescence study was performed by using a Perkin-Elmer LS-55.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of the samples, we used the standard test of water contact angle test [31]. The samples were withdrawn in a 0.5 V.% oleic acid in n-heptane solution. After 30 s, the samples were pulled out by a constant rate of 100 mm/min. To have a uniform layer of oleic acid, the samples were kept at 70 °C for 60 min. Afterwards, the sample was irradiated by a 253 nm, Hg light source and its water contact angle was measured at certain periods (3 h). According to the standard, the degradation of oleic acid will result in proportional lower hydrophobicity. The reusability performance was considered as the needing time for 90% reduction of water contact angle.

Typical characterizations of the synthesized GQDs are summarized in Fig. 2 a-e. As can be seen, a nearly wide range of particles between 6 and 25 nm is observable in the atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrograph (Fig. 2b). Also, the thickness of the GQDs is less than 3 nm (Fig. 2b). TEM image showed the average width of the GQDs in the range of 12–22 nm which nearly was in good agreement with the AFM data (see Fig. 2c). The main absorption peak of the as-prepared GQDs suspension is shown in the Fig. 1d. The peak at 240 nm is assigned to the π-π* transformation of C = C bonds in the sp2 hybridization. Furthermore, the weak shoulder appeared at 365 nm is attributed to the n- π* transition [32]. FT-IR analysis also carried out to investigate the surface of the GQDs (see Fig. 2e). The peak observed at ~ 1660 cm−1 represents the C = C groups. As expected, broad and intense peaks of residual C − O and –OH functional groups are centered at the range of 1000–1400 cm−1 and at 3400 cm−1, respectively. The GQD shows the characteristic peak of graphite centered at 2θ = 24.8◦ (JCPDS No. 41–1487) corresponding to the (002) plane which is a little lower than that of graphite (at 2θ = 26.42°) (see Fig. 2f) [32]. The larger d-spacing value of the GQDs (due to the presence of (002) plane at lower wavelengths) indicates that synthesized GQDs still contain oxygen containing functional groups and it can be related to the size and edge effects of GQDs [33].

Subsequently, the typical characterization of the synthesized UiO-66-NH2 is summarized in Fig. 3 . The size distribution and morphology of the sample are shown in Fig. 3a. Moreover, the SEM image showed that particles with irregular morphology (or quasi-spherical shape) are thoroughly aggregated and should be isolated by ultrasonication before subsequent treatment. The specific functional groups related to the UiO-66-NH2 structure are shown in the FTIR spectrum as given in Fig. 3b. The broad peak in the range of 3000–3600 cm1- is assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH groups. The characteristic bands attributed to the amine groups in UiO-66-NH2 at 3486 cm−1 (symmetric vibration of NH2) and 3375 cm−1 (asymmetric vibration of NH2) are overlapped with the broad band arising from the OH peak. Also, the other definite peaks centered at 2925, 1650, 1575, 1440, and 1385 cm−1 are assigned respectively to the vibrational modes of C − H, C = O, O-C = O (asymmetric carboxylate), C = C, and O-C = O (symmetric carboxylate) bonds. Additional peaks in the range of 425–625 cm1- are correspond to metal oxide bonds [13,34]. The characteristic band of Caromatic-N centered at 1265 cm−1 [35] is specified by the dashed lines.

The Fig. 4 a and b depicted the various steps for the deposition of UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles on an AISI 304 steel mesh. The hydroxyl groups on ZnO thin film can strongly interact with UiO-66-NH2 as explained before by Lee et al. [36] (see Fig. 4c). In fact, hydrogen bonding between amino and hydroxyl groups of the MOF and ZnO in addition to interactions between the amino groups and the Zn(II) can facilitate the deposition of UiO-66-NH2 on the thin film [36]. This issue is further confirmed by the homogenous distribution of UiO-66(Zr)–NH2 nanoparticles on the film derived from the elemental mapping analysis (Zr, O, Zn and Fe) as shown in Fig. 4d. The XRD pattern of the AISI steel/ZnO/UiO-66-NH2/GQDs is shown in Fig. 4e. Although, the UiO-66-NH2 is well crystallized according to the PXRD pattern (the inset of Fig. 4e), but the peaks of both ZnO thin film and UiO-66-NH2 MOF in the PXRD pattern of nominal solid are very weak to observe due to the strong peaks of AISI 304 steel substrate which could cover the other peaks. Fig. S1a shows XRD patterns of the AISI steel 304/ZnO, AISI steel 304/ZnO/UiO-66-NH2 and AISI steel 304/ZnO/UiO-66-NH2/GQDs. The patterns showed that the most intense diffraction peaks appear at ~ 43.5° and 51° corresponding to (111) and (200) preferential orientation of the γ phase of AISI 304 substrates [37]. The inset figure shows the characteristic diffraction peaks relating to the UiO-66-NH2 at low angles (~7.3° and 8.5°) [13,38,39]. It can be seen that after the incorporation of GQDs, the intensity of MOF related peaks is slightly decreased.

Fig. S1b depicted the FT-IR spectra of the bare UiO-66-NH2 and GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2. One can see that except of negligible differences, both of the spectra are nearly identical. As shown in the Fig. S1b, various characteristic peaks of UiO-66-NH2 such as Zr-O bond at ~ 400–650 cm−1, C = O at ~ 1650 cm−1 and other well-known peaks are presented. The broad peaks in the range of 3050–3600 cm−1 are assigned to –OH (adsorbed water and Zr-OH) and –NH2 stretching vibrations of primary amines in UiO-66-NH2. For the GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2, the peak shifts indicated the presence of hydrogen bond interactions between the primary amine groups and hydroxy groups. In addition, some GQDs characteristic peaks at about 2900 cm−1 are appeared [40]. Further, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the UiO-66-NH2 was measured and obtained to be 1100 m2/g, which is in good agreement with the previously reported surface area [28] (Fig. S2). After the incorporation of GQDs into the MOF, the BET surface area was decreased to 450 m2/g. This confirmed the successful modification and the preparation of the GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S3). The wettability of a system is a function of the cohesive forces between the solid and liquid, which obtained through the analysis of the contact angle between the horizontal (or sloping) surface of a sample and a drop of the liquid. Therefore, the effect of the deposited layers was further evaluated by measuring the water contact angle. Interestingly, the contact angle of the bare AISI 304 steel mesh was 114° which was almost twice the values reported for bare steel piece (see Fig. 5 a) [41]. This could be due to the porous structure of the structure. ZnO thin film can be hydrophilized by introducing OH functional groups. Moreover, sever water sorption property of porous UiO-66-NH2 (~0.38 [42]) could resulted in a decrement of water contact angle up to 28° (see Fig. 5b and c).

Raman spectroscopy is another useful tool for the physical characterization of the carbonaceous loaded composites. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the composite sample showed various peaks. The appeared signals at 248, 365 and around 620 cm−1 are related to the ZrO2 crystal structure in the core of UiO-66-NH2 [43]. The signals appeared at higher values are corresponded to the various forms of C- and N- based species as reported by the others [30]. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of the graphene quantum dot (D and G peaks) are appeared at 1350 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The broad peak centered at 1350 cm−1 is certainly attributed to the defect activated mode of carbonaceous structure because UiO-66-NH2 has no Raman active mode between 1300 and 1400 cm−1 [14]. Thus, due to the various types of structural defects on graphene quantum dot, d-peak is deconvoluted to 5 sub-picks, as shown in Fig. 6 . Rajender et al. [40], suggested that the best deconvolution of D-peak of GQD contains 4 district sub-peaks. As mentioned above, the main role of GQD is to survive the photoexcited electron/hole pairs on the surface of photocatalyst UiO-66-NH2. The comparative study by using PL spectra showed that GQD even in low content had a very definitive effect on this phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 6b, the UiO-66-NH2 sample exhibited two main peaks at 450 and 670 nm. Also, GQD suspension presented a strong blue luminescence. Therefore, regarding the typical electronic structure of GQD (VCB = -1.1 eV and VVB = 0.9 eV vs NHE [44]) and UiO-66-NH2 (VCB = -0.6 eV and VVB = 2.27 eV vs NHE [45]), extinction of the photoluminescence peaks excited by high energy wavelength (200 nm) of the composite sample can be explained by the following mechanisms; 1) injection of the photoexcited electrons of GQD to the conduction band of UiO-66-NH2, and 2) the photoexcited holes in the valence band of the MOF and their migration to the valence band of GQD [3]. Thus, in both of the cases, the luminescence of the composite sample is banned and resulted in the complete extinction of PL peak, even at the excitation wavelength of 200 nm.

Fig. 7 shows the photocatalytic performance of the samples against the long-chain oleic-acid molecules. As can be seen, the oleic-acid treated samples are nearly hydrophobic with the contact angle of ~ 70°. Afterward, the samples were irradiated with 253 nm and their contact angles were measured at the identical time interval of 3 h. The contact angle of the GQDs/UiO-66-NH2/ZnO/AISI steel rapidly (~10 h) decreases to lower than 5°, exhibiting a good photoinduced performance against oleic-acid. While the performance of the bare steel and ZnO/AISI steel is much lower. Concerning this issue, the mechanism of oleic acid degradation is explained as follows.

As mentioned above, the probable injection of the photoexcited electrons of GQDs to the conduction band of UiO-66-NH2 can cause an electropositivity due to the presence of extra holes which can capture the electrons from the valence band of semiconductor [3]. The remained holes interact with surrounding water vapor as:
H 2
O +
h
+
→ O
H
o
+
H
+

Besides, the freed electrons explained as the second mechanism in the PL section, can radicalize oxygen molecule as:
O 2
+
e
-
→
O
2
- o

Moreover, one must consider the effects of localized charge space around heterointerfaces between GQDs and UiO-66-NH2 which can effectively bend off the photoexcited electron/hole pairs and inhibit their recombination to ignorable photoluminescence, as shown in Fig. 6b. The Fig. 8 represents the schematic of the above mentioned reactions. Also, it was observed just 10% reduction after three times cycling test (inset of Fig. 8).

Rathusky et al. [25], reported that primary attack of these radicals on the organic matter not only resulted in a decrease of the adhesion of the organic layer on the substrate, but also degraded oleic acid to hydrophilic species of nonanal, 9-oxononanoic acid, azelaic, and nonanoic acid.
According to our knowledge, in accordance to the ISO:27448 standard test, the performance of GQDs/UiO-66-NH2 composite layer outperforms better than the others [25,26], as mentioned before.

The graphene quantum dot incorporated UiO-66-NH2 photocatalyst was successfully deposited on ZnO precoated AISI 304 steel mesh by a facile procedure. The ZnO thin film could serve as an interface layer for better mass-loading and distribution of UiO-66-NH2 through heterojunctions. This subject was confirmed by elemental mapping of Zr and Zn atoms and resulted in a comparative hydrophilicity. In the next step, photosensitivity of the MOF was upgraded by addition of graphene quantum dot (GQD). The photosensitizer GQD had an outstanding effect on the photocatalytic performance of UiO-66-NH2 and degraded completely the oleic-acid after 9 h of ultraviolet irradiation which much less than that of the sample without GQD. This observation was ascribed to the photoinduced charge transfer between UiO-66-NH2 and graphene quantum dot. PL peaks for GQDs and UiO-66-NH2 are quite faded in the composite. Thus, the GQDs incorporated UiO-66-NH2 MOF have potential applications for the continuous removal of pollutants from real wastewater.

M.K.; Conceptualization. S.S. and A.R.O.; Methodology, Software, Validation and co-wrote; S.S, A.R.O. and R.A. done the characterization and wrote the characterization section of manuscript, M. K. who supervised the project and wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank from the Iran National Science Foundation for ﬁnancial support.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
